title: America's political economy is pretty bleak right now
gist: Noah Smith argues the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act's bipartisan passage shows Matt Yglesias's 'Secret Congress' theory still works for low-profile reform, but that America's deeper political economy — split among an unproductive MAGA right, socialist left, and 'woke' left — makes any bold, sweeping policy program unlikely, drawing an explicit (if partial) analogy to the Spanish Civil War.

themes.theme: The ROAD to Housing Act as a 'Secret Congress' win

insights.claim: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act became law after passing the House and Senate with overwhelming bipartisan majorities despite Trump's refusal to sign it, containing expedited/simplified environmental review for housing projects, more federal funding for jurisdictions that build more housing (and less for those that build less), and deregulation of prefab home construction and housing finance.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A concrete, bipartisan federal YIMBY-style housing supply reform has actually passed — watch for downstream effects on housing construction financing and permitting timelines despite the federal government's otherwise limited housing authority.
insights.locator: Consider the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act

insights.claim: The Act includes a 'slopulist' provision restricting corporate landlords from buying single-family homes, based on the myth (believed across the political spectrum from Elizabeth Warren to Donald Trump) that corporate/private-equity investors are responsible for the housing crisis; in reality corporate landlords are a tiny sliver of the market and some evidence suggests they reduce rents, especially for low-income tenants, so the provision's carve-outs (e.g., for corporations that build-to-rent) mean it will likely have minimal real-world impact.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A politically popular but economically hollow provision — investors in single-family rental / build-to-rent operators should not expect material earnings impact from this restriction given the carve-outs.
insights.locator: Notably, the bill also contains one unhelpful "slopulist" provision

insights.claim: The Act's passage validates Matt Yglesias's 'Secret Congress' theory: legislators mostly want to do what's good for the country but are blocked by partisan electorate anger, so good laws pass only when they stay low-profile and avoid becoming a politicized 'food fight.'
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Predicts that future substantive US legislative progress will keep coming from low-salience, technical bills rather than headline reforms — a lens for spotting which pending bills are actually likely to pass.
insights.locator: It’s a validation of Matt Yglesias’ “Secret Congress” theory

insights.claim: Because 'Secret Congress' dynamics require bills to remain low-profile to pass, dedicated public servants can only 'tinker around the edges' and cannot enact the kind of bold, sweeping policy programs that FDR, Reagan, and (to a lesser degree) Obama were able to carry out.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Sets expectations that no major, headline-grabbing US economic policy overhaul (fiscal, industrial, or AI policy) is likely to pass in the current environment — bet on incrementalism, not transformation.
insights.locator: But this scope is very limited, because “Secret Congress” has to remain secret

insights.claim: America's political economy is dominated by three major movements — the MAGA right, the socialist left, and the 'woke' left — none of which is especially interested in smart, reasonable policies that would materially improve regular Americans' material well-being; their economic programs are all weak, and even if persuadable toward better policy, inter-movement conflict keeps them focused on culture-war issues.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames the core investable political risk as gridlock/culture-war distraction rather than any single faction's economic program — implies persistent policy uncertainty and low odds of coherent large-scale fiscal or industrial policy shifts.
insights.locator: Roughly speaking, we have three major political movements

themes.theme: The Spanish Civil War analogy for US political risk

insights.claim: Smith has drawn an analogy between America's political trajectory and the Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) since 2016, and found it increasingly apt through nationwide riots, a coup attempt, military purges, election-theft accusations, and increasingly authoritarian politics — while stating explicitly he does not think the US will have an actual civil war, since that would require the military to fragment as it did in Spain, which he considers unlikely.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Provides Smith's explicit risk framework for US political instability — an actual military fracture is the key tail-risk marker he'd watch for before taking 'civil war' scenarios seriously, and he currently rates that as unlikely.
insights.locator: In the years since, as America suffered nationwide riots

insights.claim: In the Spanish Civil War, Spain was riven by deep class conflict; a 1934 leftist miners' strike-turned-revolution attempt was crushed but panicked rightists into fearing future election losses would trigger revolution, leading them to stage a coup in 1936 after a center-left coalition won; nationalists slowly ground down the republic over three years while the left fractured internally, and neither side had a workable governing program (Spain stayed poor for decades under the victorious rightists).
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The generalizable lesson Smith draws is that a society can fracture into factions where NO side has a workable governing program yet people still fight hard out of fear, ideology, and opportunism — his implicit warning about current US politics regardless of which faction 'wins.'
insights.locator: Here’s a very brief two-paragraph synopsis of the Spanish Civil War

insights.claim: Smith maps the Spanish Civil War onto US politics via a four-step pattern: (1) a long-standing intractable social conflict flares into a moment of mass revolutionary fervor, (2) that scare panics right-wing factions into uniting behind a radical/authoritarian program, (3) the most revolutionary left faction gets muscled out by the most disciplined faction which then loses, (4) the eventual right-wing victors have no workable governing program and keep the country poor for decades.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A structured mental model for tracking US political escalation stages — investors can use this as a checklist for how close current events (e.g., a 'revolutionary fervor' moment, faction consolidation) map to the analogy's more dangerous later stages.
insights.locator: So how do I see the Spanish Civil War as analogous
